title: Lecture Template
text:
Introduction
These slides are created using revealjs format in Quarto.

We’ve highlighted many of the useful and cool features, but you can explore even more on the Quarto website:

revealjs (Presentations in Quarto)
Authoring in Quarto (markdown basics & content formatting)
Quarto: The Definitive Guide (a book about Quarto created using Quarto

title: Lecture Template
text:
Math
You can write mathematical equations using LaTex

e.g. $f(y) = x^2$ renders to \(f(y) = x^2\)

You can center math on its own line using double dollar signs, e.g. $$f(y) = x^2$$ renders to:
\[f(y) = x^2\]

Syntax

For emphasizing key equations

title: Lecture Template
text:
Animated Bullets
You can create incremental lists using the incremental div.


Source
::: incremental
-   la
-   la
-   land
:::

Rendered

la
la
land



Note: You can also enable this for all lists by default:
format:
  revealjs:
    incremental: true

title: Lecture Template
text:
Writing Code
To include runnable code in Quarto, use fenced code blocks with the appropriate language engine:

```{r}
# Load data
data(mtcars)

# Fit linear model
model <- lm(mpg ~ wt + hp, data = mtcars)
```


Use three backticks followed by {r} to start an R code block.
You can add Chunk Options after #|, one per line.

title: Lecture Template
text:
Chunk Options
These control how code blocks behave:

echo: true/false – Show or hide the source code.
eval: true/false – Evaluate the code or not.
code-fold: true/false – Allow the code block to be collapsible.
code-summary: "text" – Customize label for folded code.



These are just a few popular examples—Quarto supports more chunk options.
➡️ Full reference: https://quarto.org/docs/computations/execution-options.html

title: Lecture Template
text:
Example: Code Folding
See the next slide for the rendered output …
```{r}
#| echo: true
#| code-summary: "Click me to show the code!"
#| code-fold: true

library(ggplot2)
dat <- data.frame(cond = rep(c("A", "B"), each=10),
                  xvar = 1:20 + rnorm(20,sd=3),
                  yvar = 1:20 + rnorm(20,sd=3))

ggplot(dat, aes(x=xvar, y=yvar)) +
  geom_point(shape=1) + 
  geom_smooth() 
```

title: Lecture Template
text:
Example: Code Folding


Click me to show the code!
library(ggplot2)
dat <- data.frame(cond = rep(c("A", "B"), each=10),
                  xvar = 1:20 + rnorm(20,sd=3),
                  yvar = 1:20 + rnorm(20,sd=3))

ggplot(dat, aes(x=xvar, y=yvar)) +
  geom_point(shape=1) + 
  geom_smooth()

title: Lecture Template
text:
Example: Code Hiding
Setting echo: false hides the code and shows only the output.
```{r}
#| echo: false

library(ggplot2)
dat <- data.frame(cond = rep(c("A", "B"), each=10),
                  xvar = 1:20 + rnorm(20,sd=3),
                  yvar = 1:20 + rnorm(20,sd=3))

ggplot(dat, aes(x=xvar, y=yvar)) +
  geom_point(shape=1) + 
  geom_smooth() 
```
See the next slide for the rendered output …

title: Lecture Template
text: Example: Code Hiding

title: Lecture Template
text:
Code Copying
All code blocks include a clipboard icon by default. Hover and click to copy the code.

library(ggplot2)
dat <- data.frame(cond = rep(c("A", "B"), each=10),
                  xvar = 1:20 + rnorm(20,sd=3),
                  yvar = 1:20 + rnorm(20,sd=3))

ggplot(dat, aes(x=xvar, y=yvar)) +
  geom_point(shape=1) + 
  geom_smooth()

title: Lecture Template
text:
Output Location
By default, the output will follow immediately after the code. Use output-location to control where the output appears:







output-location
Description




fragment
Output appears as a fragment—revealed when you advance the slide


slide
Output appears on the next slide, separate from the code


column
Code and output shown side‑by‑side in two equal-width columns on one slide


column-fragment
Same as column, but output is delayed as a fragment

title: Lecture Template
text:
Adjusting Slide Size
The table didn’t quite fit on the previous slide, so we use the .smaller CSS class to reduce the font size for the entire slide. Here’s what’s happening under the hood:
## Normal Slide

This is normal size text.

## Smaller Slide {.smaller}

This text will be smaller so more fits on the slide.

title: Lecture Template
text:
Example: Default (output after code)

ggplot(dat, aes(x=xvar, y=yvar)) +
  geom_point(shape=1) + 
  geom_smooth()

title: Lecture Template
text:
Example: Output on next slide

```{r}
#| output-location: slide

ggplot(dat, aes(x=xvar, y=yvar)) +
  geom_point(shape=1) + 
  geom_smooth() 
```

title: Lecture Template
text: Example: Output on next slide

title: Lecture Template
text:
Example: Column


```{r}
#| output-location: column

ggplot(dat, aes(x=xvar, y=yvar)) +
  geom_point(shape=1) + 
  geom_smooth() 
```

title: Lecture Template
text:
Example: Resize figure


```{r}
#| output-location: column
#| fig-width: 4
#| fig-height: 3
#| out-width: "100%"

ggplot(dat, aes(x=xvar, y=yvar)) +
  geom_point(shape=1) + 
  geom_smooth() 
```

title: Lecture Template
text:
Scrollable Slides
The .scrollable class makes overflow content scrollable vertically rather than being cut off.
You can acheive it using:
## Slide Title {.scrollable}
This is especially useful when you have:

Long code blocks
Large tables
Verbose output
Lists or explanations that extend beyond the slide height

title: Lecture Template
text:
Smaller Slides
The .smaller class—used in the same way as .scrollable— reduces the base font size for the entire slide, excluding the slide title.
## Normal Slide

This is normal size text.

## Smaller Slide {.smaller}

This text will be smaller so more fits on the slide.
An example of this can be seen on this slide.

title: Lecture Template
text:
Cross-referencing slides

Given the following slide title, Quarto will automatically generate the ID: of my-amazing-slide.1
## My Amazing Slide! 
To reference that slide we can use

[this slide](#my-amazing-slide), e.g. this slide
[My Amazing Slide!], e.g. Output Location


Special characters are removed, spaces become dashes, and uppercase letters are converted to lowercase.

title: Lecture Template
text:
Custom Titles
If you want to make a custom-id, use:
## This slide title is too long {#short-id}
and reference using:
[link](#short-id) 

Quarto auto-generates heading IDs (like Pandoc) by: converting to lower case, removing punctation and replacing spaces with hyphens
How to manually assign IDs using {#my-id}
How these IDs can be used as anchors
This slide is here to show how to make custom slide ids

There are five types of callouts, including: note, warning, important, tip, and caution; see Callout Blocks

If you do not specify a title it will default to the name of the callout type.

A “minimal” appearance important callout (supresses icon)

title: Lecture Template
text:
Whats Ahead
The above should be more than you need to get started creating presentations in your course. If you’re building more customized or interactive slides, we cover:

Fragments & animations
Slide state events
Custom keyboard bindings
Code execution and plugins
Embedding other HTML/JS content

title: Lecture Template
text:
Code highlighting
In addition to the HTML Code Block options, revealjs supports the ability to do line highlighting. This is accomplished using:
#| code-line-numbers: "1-2|4-5|7-8"

# Load data
data(mtcars)

# Fit linear model
model <- lm(mpg ~ wt + hp, data = mtcars)

# Summary output
summary(model)  



See Quarto: Line Highlighting

title: Lecture Template
text:
Code Annotation
Much like footnotes, we could use Code Annotations to attach explanation to lines of code.

library(tidyverse)
library(palmerpenguins)
1penguins |>
2  mutate(
    bill_ratio = bill_depth_mm / bill_length_mm,
    bill_area  = bill_depth_mm * bill_length_mm
  )


1

Take penguins, and then,

2

add new columns for the bill ratio and bill area.






Source: https://quarto.org/docs/authoring/code-annotation.html

title: Lecture Template
Lecture Template
text:
Code Animations II
You can also animate between code blocks to show changes in code. For example:
# Define a server for the Shiny app
function(input, output) {
  
  # Fill in the spot we created for a plot
  output$phonePlot <- renderPlot({
    # Render a barplot
    barplot(WorldPhones[,input$region]*1000, 
            main=input$region,
            ylab="Number of Telephones",
            xlab="Year")
  })
}

Learn more: Code Animations

title: Lecture Template
text:
Visual Editor
The Visual Editor (VE) (as opposed to the Source Editor) in RStudio is a WYSIWYM editor interface.
It includes a toolbar with buttons and menu options for:

formatting text (bold, italics, headers, lists)
inserting tables, images
hyperlinks,
and more!

Bonus: It also provides previews of rendered math, plots, and supports the execution of code cells.

title: Lecture Template
text:
Figures/Images using VE
Quarto supports a number of ways to include figures and subfigures into your documents; see Quarto:Figures.

This is how you can included an image in yours slides using the Visual Editor in RStudio

title: Lecture Template
text:
Figure Options

The VE will open up a pop-up window where you can choose the alignment of your image (default, left, center, right), add captions, and include alternative text.

title: Lecture Template
text:
Right-aligned Image
For demonstration we make our image left-aligned.

In the Source Editor, the markdown syntax is:
![](img/logo.png){fig-align="right"}

title: Lecture Template
Lecture Template
text:
Resizing in Visual Editor

In VE you can resize an image by clicking and dragging its corner handles.
By default, the aspect ratio is locked. You can disable this and adjust dimensions independently.
See the next slide for a demonstration

title: Lecture Template
text:
Videos
You can embed videos in documents using {{< video >}} the shortcode; see Quarto: videos

For example, here we embed a YouTube video (see the rendered output on the following slide):
{{< video https://youtu.be/EbAAmrB0luA?si=f0TtOI9v_kotj8bk 
  width="800" 
  aspect-ratio="21x12" 
  >}}

title: Lecture Template
text:
iFrame
You can also embed videos/webpages using an iFrame








Note


Some websites block iframe, so previews may not appear in the rendered Quarto output for those sites.

title: Regression
text:
Introduction


In this lecture we will introduce Simple Linear Regression (SLR) to model the relationship between two numerical variables.
We will quantify the relationship between variables using popular metrics like the Correlation Coefficient and \(R^2\) value.
Statistical inference for this model will be discussed, and the critical distinction between inference for mean response and inference for the outcome will be clarified.
We will also introduce a basic understanding of the multiple regression model.

title: Regression
text:
Recall: Scatterplots

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.

title: Regression
text: Line of Best fit

title: Regression
text: Visualization of Residuals

title: Regression
text:
What is regression

Regression analysis is a tool to investigate how two or more variables are related.

“The general objective of a regression analysis is to determine the relationship between two (or more) variables so that we can gain information about one of them through knowing values of the other(s).”


Source: Ch 12 of Devore, Berk, and Carlton (2021)

title: Regression
text:
Variable Types
We want to see how a specific response variable, \(Y\) is affected by one or more explanatory variables \(X_1, \dots, X_p\)

\(Y\) is also known as the dependent variable or output variable. This variable is continuous (i.e. numeric data type)
\(X_i\)’s are also known as independent/predictor variables or features. These may be continuous or categorical; however, we only consider continuous predictors in this course.

title: Regression
text:
Example



One may wish to use a person’s height, gender and race to predict a person’s weight. What is/are the response variable(s)?





Height
Gender
Race
Weight

title: Regression
text:
Example



One may wish to use a person’s height, gender and race to predict a person’s weight. What is/are the dependent variable(s)?





Height
Gender
Race
Weight

title: Regression
Regression
text:
Model
\[\begin{align}
Y &= \underbrace{f(X_1, \dots, X_p)}_{\text{deterministic}} + \underbrace{\epsilon}_{\text{random}}\\
&= \beta_0 + \beta_1 X_1 + \beta_2 X_2 + \dots \beta_p X_p + \epsilon
\end{align}\]

In a linear regression model, the function \(f(X_1, \dots, X_p)\) is a linear combination of the explanatory variables.

title: Regression
text:
Model
\[\begin{align}
\overset{\overset{\text{response}}{\downarrow}}{Y} &= \beta_0 + \beta_1 \underset{\underset{\text{explanatory}}{\uparrow}}{X_1} + \beta_2 \underset{\underset{\text{explanatory}}{\uparrow}}{X_2} + \dots \beta_p \underset{\underset{\text{explanatory}}{\uparrow}}{X_p} + \epsilon
\end{align}\]


\(\beta_0\) is the \(y\)-intercept
\(\beta_i\) are the regression coefficients, and
\(\epsilon\) are the errors that are assumed \(N(0, \sigma)\)



In statistics, we can describe how variables are related using a mathematical function.
The function along with other assumptions is called a model.
There are many models we can consider. In this class, we will focus on linear models, particularly, when there is only one predictor variable.
We refer to this model as the simple linear regression model. (Penn STat 500)


as well as modeling numerical response variables using a numerical or categorical explanatory variable.

title: Regression
text:
Simple Linear Regression

When \(p = 1\), namely, we are only considering one explanatory variable, our model is called “simple” linear regression (SLR).
When \(p>1\) we fall into multiple linear regression (MLR).

title: Regression
text:
SLR model
The SLR model reduces to an equation for of a line:
\[Y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X\]
where:

\(\beta_0\) is the \(y\)-intercept, and
\(\beta_1\) is the slope

title: Regression
text:
\(X\)sParametersDeterministicNoise


Create n values for x, for example using:

x = runif(n, min = -2, max = 10) # or runif() / rnorm()












For \(\beta_0\) = beta0 = 3, \(\beta_1\) = beta1 = 4, we have
\[Y = 3 + 4 \cdot X\]

title: Regression
text:
References




Devore, J. L., K. N. Berk, and M. A. Carlton. 2021. Modern Mathematical Statistics with Applications. Springer Texts in Statistics. Springer International Publishing. https://books.google.ca/books?id=ghcsEAAAQBAJ.
